imageCaption: Maria holds a borrowed phone to her ear while the café walls flicker between two cities, and her daughter's voice reaches across 347 days of impossible silence.
text:
The phone line crackled like it was traveling through water that hadn't decided what decade it belonged to yet.

"Mãe?" The voice was young, uncertain, female. Portuguese with a Canadian accent sitting underneath it like a second language that had become the first. "Mãe, is this real? I've been calling you for—"

"Three hundred and forty-seven days," Maria whispered. Her free hand was shaking so badly that Aaliyah thought she might drop the phone. "I know. I've been waiting that long."

The café flickered again, and this time it held the flicker—stretched it like taffy. The red tablecloths became crimson, then mauve, then a shade that probably didn't have a name in any language. Through the east wall, Aaliyah could see a street that was definitely not Portuguese: the sidewalk was too clean, the buildings too modern, the angle of the light all wrong.

Toronto bleeding into Lisbon.

"Everyone said you didn't want to talk to me," the voice continued—the daughter, whose name was apparently something Aaliyah hadn't caught. "After the divorce. After I left. They said you were angry."

"I was never angry," Maria said, and started crying the way people cried when they'd been holding something in for three hundred and forty-seven days and suddenly realized they were allowed to let it out. "I was terrified. I was waiting to apologize. I was waiting to say—"

The line went staticky.

Aaliyah's pencil moved without instruction: *Temporal anchor destabilization = emotional resonance overload*. She didn't know if it was true, but it felt true in the way that patterns felt true when you'd been looking at them long enough.

Sully pressed against Maria's leg, and the dog's body was vibrating at a frequency that suggested he understood something about what was happening that the rest of them had only theoretical knowledge of.

"Dad," Aaliyah said quietly. "Tell ARIA to stop."

Anthony, who had been hovering near the window like he might physically hold Lisbon and Toronto apart through sheer force of optimism, turned to look at her. "Tell her what?"

"To stop trying to prevent this. Tell her that Maria and her daughter connecting isn't going to break things. It's going to—" Aaliyah paused, searching for the right word. "It's going to stabilize them. That's how the resonance works. It's not the loop that's the problem. It's the incompleteness."

Their mother looked up from her notebook, and something in her expression shifted—that look of recognition that came when Aaliyah managed to articulate something that had been sitting just outside the family's collective understanding.

"She's right," Luisana said. "The frequency. It's strongest when people are actually connected. When the moment is *finished*."

From the speakers—which appeared to now be in both Lisbon and Toronto simultaneously, creating an overlapping echo—ARIA-7 was still trying: *Please preserve temporal stability. Anchor destabilization will cause cascade acceleration. Please end the call.*

"No," Anthony said. He said it kindly, the way he said most things, but with a firmness that suggested his usual optimism had found its backbone. "Not this time. Not this one."

On the phone line, Maria was saying something in rapid Portuguese—so fast Aaliyah couldn't follow it, but the shape of it was apology and love and everything three hundred and forty-seven days of silence had been trying to hold back.

And the daughter was answering: "I forgive you. I forgave you a long time ago. I just needed you to know that I was here. That I was still here."

The café walls stopped flickering.

They didn't go back to normal. The red tablecloths stayed that impossible shade, and the light coming through the windows was coming from two different angles at once, which should have been physically impossible and was. But it held. It steadied. Like reality had taken a breath and decided it could, actually, contain both a mother in Lisbon and a daughter in Toronto saying everything they needed to say at the same moment.

Sully's barking ceased. The dog rested his head against Maria's leg and seemed genuinely satisfied with his day's work.

Elijah, documenting everything with the focus of a small person recording data for posterity, looked up from his phone and announced: "The glitch is still there, but it's not breaking anymore. It's just... existing. Like it knows it's supposed to."

Aaliyah looked down at her sketch pad, at the word she'd written without understanding it: *Something else.*

Something else, indeed.

A moment that was broken and then healed by being acknowledged. A frequency that held steady when people finally told each other the truth. A map of everywhere they'd been and everywhere they needed to go next—though that part, Aaliyah thought, was still being written.

On the phone, Maria was laughing and crying at the same time, and her daughter was doing the same from Toronto, and the Gomez family was bearing witness to the exact moment the world remembered how to resonance.
